summary: Jeff Atwood discusses a Microsoft MVP who created .NET code capable of defeating MSDN's visual CAPTCHA system. The author demonstrated his crack by comment spamming 94 fellow MVPs' blogs, then issued a half-apologetic, half-boastful explanation. Atwood notes the technique exploits the continuous lines of contrast between CAPTCHA characters and backgrounds. While finding the code technically interesting, Atwood clearly disapproves of the demonstration method, calling the author 'kind of a jerk' for spam-testing on real people's blogs.
key_points: CAPTCHA systems can be defeated programmatically by analyzing visual contrast patterns between characters and backgrounds
The MVP chose to demonstrate his exploit by actually spamming real blogs rather than using a controlled test environment
Security measures like CAPTCHA can provide a 'false sense of security' when they're breakable
There's an ethical dimension to security research - proving a vulnerability exists doesn't require exploiting real users
money_quotes: So, yeah, the author is kind of a jerk.
And he has some problems with capitalization and punctuation, too.
your blog uses CAPTCHA to provide a false sense of security
this is just my way of saying that we've got more work to do
key_insight: Interesting security research can be undermined by poor judgment in how vulnerabilities are demonstrated, and proving you can break something doesn't justify actually breaking it on unsuspecting users.
